title: When irrelevant alternatives do matter. The effect of focusing on loan decisions
summary_en: In a loan-choice experiment, does adding an irrelevant third option change which of two loans people pick — and in what direction? The answer is yes: a clearly dominated decoy shifts preferences, consistent with salience / focusing-based models of choice. The authors develop a model where decision-makers over-weight dimensions on which options differ most, predict the direction of the induced preference reversal, and match it experimentally. A crisp test of focusing effects with direct implications for consumer financial-product design.
data_used: Laboratory experiment on loan-choice decisions with a manipulated irrelevant third option. Subject pool and payoffs calibrated to a standard economic-experiments protocol.
policy_relevance: Direct input for MNB / Pénzügyminisztérium consumer-finance disclosure regulation: the layout of loan alternatives on a comparison page is not neutral. Supports rules that restrict dominated 'decoy' products from being shown alongside real options. Caveat: laboratory setting; field validation in actual loan-product pages would strengthen the policy case.
